en.bodyHowToPlay:
Click on the pins to open the way for the matter particles and for the fish.

Click on an active water valve to release more water when it is possible.

Take the fish to the exit to win the level!
en.titleEnableAutomaticTTS: Do you wish to enable automatic text to speech?
en.titleWrongAnswer: You were caught! But don't worry.
en.bodyPlayAgainWarning: You can play levels again if you want to earn 3 stars!
en.bodyDefeatLava: Yeah, you're right. The lava is a Liquid, but a VERY HOT one. But don't worry.
en.bodyDefeatBurned: It is better to avoid being too close to this plasma stuff. But don't worry.
en.bodyDefeatFrozen: Brrr! I'm too cold for this. But don't worry.
en.bodyDefeatOutOfAir: Breathing is important, you know? But don't worry.
en.titleCutscene_Fish_1: I'm tired of living inside this aquarium... I want to see the OCEAN!
en.titleCutscene_Fish_2: If only I understood more about how states of matter work, I would be able to find a way out of here...
en.titleCutscene_Fish_3: Maybe I can jump inside that liquid water in the bathtub. This surely is a good idea!
en.titleCutsceneFinal_Fish_1: Now I know so much about states of matter!
en.titleCutsceneFinal_Fish_2: I can finally enjoy swiming on this liquid water from the ocean...
en.titleCutsceneFinal_Fish_3: I think I will go to the North Pole... this surely is a good idea!
en.titleLastLevelDialogue_Fish_1: It is time to prove that I learned everything about states of matter!
en.titleLastLevelDialogue_Fish_2: And that I don't like fire.
en.bodyTutorial_level1: Correct! Gravity leads the water towards a body of water during Runoff. In this case, it was the ocean!
en.bodyTutorial_level2: The liquid shape is now different! The volume is the same. A liquid flows to take the shape of its container.
en.bodyTutorial_waterShape_FailState1: Remember! Liquids will flow to take the shape of their container. Open up the way!
en.bodyTutorial_waterShape_FailState2: Remember! Liquids will flow to take the shape of their container. Close the way!
en.bodyTutorial_level3: Did you notice the water particles moving all together until the end? In a liquid, particles of matter move relative to one another, but remain in contact. See how they interact below.
en.bodyQuestion_level_4: After the liquid water dropped from top to bottom, what happened to its volume?
en.bodyTutorial_level5: This is a cold emitter. It will affect the state of any matter and make it REALLY COLD. Matter may change its state based on conditions such as temperature and pressure.
en.bodyQuestion_level_5: You saw water changing from liquid to solid. This was due to:
en.bodyTutorial_level6:
Different from a liquid, a solid maintains its shape and volume. That's why the Fish can go above it. It is generally denser than a liquid and its particles are closely spaced but still vibrate in place! 

Ice is less dense than water, that's why ice cubes float! It's a pretty cool exception!
en.bodyTutorial_level7: When exposed to heat, liquid water becomes vapor (gas) and it expands to take the volume of the container. When vapor is exposed to cold temperatures, it will become liquid again, and it will drop, since a liquid is generally more dense than a gas.
en.bodyQuestion_level_8: What happens to the volume of water if it's in a liquid state and something heats it up?
en.bodyTutorial_level9: There are four states of matter. Here you can see all of them at the same time!
en.bodyQuestion_level_9: Which one of these is NOT considered a state of matter?
en.bodyTutorial_level11: Vapor is water in gas state. When matter is in gas state, its particles move relative to one another and do not remain in contact. They are widely spaced except when they collide, as you can see below.
en.bodyQuestion_level_11: Compared to gas particles, liquid particles are:
en.bodyQuestion_level_12: Atoms and molecules are in constant motion. Is this true even for solids, like the lava rock you just saw?
en.08030df1-1463-4129-8143-a88c6f84e6e8: This is my text. It is a cool text test. I will test this text until the end of testing text time.
en.d056613f-3b39-4b2c-baf9-6dec4275e70b: This is a test text. A text test writen to test my texts. This dialogue popup will be displayed when we want.
en.ea66f846-fbdf-42fc-8e2c-fe0cc521f568: During runoff water tends to move towards a body of water. You just saw this happening, where did the water go to?
en.722385ee-f9d6-4131-9b16-58dc3d77c979: Complete your task and this screen will be closed!
en.a51d0118-aed7-40e8-8eca-1e0e39c5531d: Do your task now! Lol, what are you waiting for?
en.7e79da4c-224d-45d8-807c-e825d0e95133:
Hey! This is a learning information we see on the beggingn of a level.
We can type here and there!!!

Do spaces, enter lines etc.
en.724090b3-8c18-4bb8-bd0d-048fc50d3ca9: Test question, do you know this question?
en.2fc9db45-2de1-4705-a5f0-564b7ddb8e0d: Como se fala uai em gaúcho?
en.ec91727b-0c54-4d3f-a5b5-e0603d930b6b: All right, I’m all set up!
en.c22c8341: All right, I’m all set up!
en.ae894597: Fire Academy was hard but I’m excited for my first day as a firefighter!
en.f52f6b7d: Hey there, welcome! I see you’re ready to start!
en.817a2481: Oh no! Seems like a fire broke out in the city! Quick, take this to try to stop it!
en.19dfe2bf: It's an experimental water and foam jet! We are not sure which one is better, so pay attention to that while you use it!
en.41be57b7: What did you notice while putting out the fires?
en.41be57b7_answer_0: One substance seemed to put out fires faster
en.41be57b7_answer_1: Both substances take the same time to put out fires
en.41be57b7_answer_2: None of the substances managed to put out any fires
en.da2c2123: Oh wow, you really got them good! I got to be honest, I thought you would be toast by now.
en.62adbf46: But I noticed something curious! Do you know what it was?
en.45e33f60: But I noticed something curious! Do you know what it was
en.5a77791f: That’s right, it did seem like one substance was better than the other
en.d4908fc0: Hum… that doesn’t seem right, I saw one substance putting out fires faster
en.19edc2e5: But we need to be sure! And I know just the way, with the Scientific Method!
en.5cfd2748: The Scientific Method is a way to check if something is true or not, through a set of steps.
en.181f8b40: The Scientific Method starts with a question, which is what we just had. We asked ourselves what we noticed putting the fire out.
en.b263d493: Now, the next step is a hypothesis, which basically is a guess about the answer to a scientific question.
en.d5e5c154: Sometimes scientists actually have a hypothesis before an experiment, so I think we should try that too!
en.b6e5ecfe: What do you think makes more sense?
en.b6e5ecfe_answer_0: Foam puts out fires faster than water
en.b6e5ecfe_answer_1: Water puts out fires faster than foam
en.b6e5ecfe_answer_2: The fire disappears faster on its own
en.f2ba4dba: We shall put it to the test then, we’ll see if you’re right. Our plan is to use both and check! Let’s carry that out next tim-
en.bfabc5e1: Well, that’s convenient…although it’s still a fire. Come on rookie, go get them! And don’t forget to observe!
en.e0c2807f: Well, that’s convenient…although it’s still a fire. Come on rookie, go get them! And don’t forget to observe!
en.90e97584: Very good! Seems like we are going in the right direction. Now we need to find a way to test our question.
en.eb083494: A testable question is one you can answer with observations in a Experiment, the next step of the Scientific Method.
en.562e2356: But when you’re making an experiment, it’s important to check that the question is actually testable.
en.d8c6103c: Something else that is really important is to understand the variables. They are basically a part of the experiment that is observed, changed, or both.
en.6e5553f9: With all that said, let’s prepare for the experiment! What do you think we will need?
en.6a0cbe1d: Click on what you think it's needed for the experiment
en.08a68c2b: Yeah, sounds like you thought of everything! I actually know of a place where you can get all of that-
en.51b0c70a: This is getting ridiculous! The place where the materials are is on fire too! You know what to do, put that fire out and get the materials!
en.8dc96888: Look at that! You did it again kiddo! And got all the materials to boot!
en.f597a19b: Okay, so we need to decide what we will observe in this experiment. I believe that what we need to observe is the time it takes for the fire to be put out with foam or water.
en.8f590586: So the time it takes for the fire to be put out is a kind of quantitative data, because it can be counted and measured precisely.
en.552f941a: Data that can’t be measured precisely but can be described is called Qualitative Data, by the way.
en.86984d3c: That being said, we should start preparing the trials. What is a trial you say? It is a single test of what happens to the variables during an experiment.
en.d25fe196: Most experiments have more than one trial, so we better have at least two as well. All right, this is it. Let’s begin!
en.67486d7e: Ah, look at that! We managed to observe a lot! And I got it all written down in this table.
en.9c14138c: I think it’s pretty clear, but our conclusion is that “Foam puts down fires quicker than water”.
en.6a7f0924: Good! We will be sure to use that knowledge next tim-
en.069eb7a1: Oh, this is it kiddo! Now that we know foam is better, I’ll give you all the foam we got! Go show them who’s boss!
en.39bc8762: They are delicious! But not what we need...
en.7e55592e: Oh no that hammer is dangerous! I don't think we need it right now.
en.7b6e4546: Maybe if we had time to make a salad...but not for the experiment.
en.6b89864a: That's my favorite mug! But it's not very usefull right now
en.1e56aaa8: Click on the boxes to start
en.4e2a1921: Water puts out fires faster than foam
en.233d4626: QUALITATIVE DATA = CAN NOT BE COUNTED OR TIMED
en.3c502ff9: QUALITATIVE DATA = CAN NOT BE COUNTED OR TIMED
en.823a09d6: Well, since it’s your first day you won't need to do much, just get used to everything and rela-
en.9dfaf8d6: We can now analyze this and figure out what it means! This is also part of the Scientific Method by the way. But just to be sure, let's make one more trial!
en.10fcd887: Well, I think we’re ready for the last step of the Scientific Method, the conclusion! In the conclusion, we will compare our prediction during the investigation to the results we had.
en.loadingHint1: Your backpack targets fires automatically when you get close!
en.loadingHint2: Don't get too close to the fire, you might hurt yourself.
en.loadingHint3: If an enemy has an item you'll get it automatically when you defeat it.
en.loadingHint4: Sir Francis Bacon, is considered the father of the Scientific Method. But many other important people inspired him.
en.firstFire: Look, that fire creature is causing damage to the city! We must put it out
en.tutorialUpstairsFire: Look, there are fires on top of that building! Go put them out!

es.bodyDefeatBurned: Es mejor evitar estar demasiado cerca de este material de plasma. Pero no te preocupes.
es.bodyDefeatFrozen: ¡Brrr! Tengo demasiado frío para esto. Pero no te preocupes.
es.bodyDefeatLava: Sí, tienes razón. La lava es líquida, pero MUY CALIENTE. Pero no te preocupes.
es.bodyDefeatOutOfAir: Respirar es importante, ¿sabes? Pero no te preocupes.
es.bodyHowToPlay:
Haz clic en los alfileres para abrir el camino a las partículas de materia y a los peces.

Haga clic en una válvula de agua activa para liberar más agua cuando sea posible.

¡Lleva a los peces a la salida para ganar el nivel!
es.bodyPlayAgainWarning: ¡Puedes volver a jugar niveles si quieres ganar 3 estrellas!
es.bodyQuestion_level_11: En comparación con las partículas de gas, las partículas líquidas son:
es.bodyQuestion_level_12: Los átomos y las moléculas están en constante movimiento. ¿Esto es cierto incluso para los sólidos, como la roca de lava que acabas de ver?
es.bodyQuestion_level_4: Después de que el agua líquida cayera de arriba a abajo, ¿qué pasó con su volumen?
es.bodyQuestion_level_5: Viste que el agua cambiaba de líquida a sólida. Esto se debió a:
es.bodyQuestion_level_8: ¿Qué pasa con el volumen de agua si está en estado líquido y algo lo calienta?
es.bodyQuestion_level_9: ¿Cuál de estos NO se considera un estado de la materia?
es.bodyTutorial_level1: Haz clic en cualquier pin para abrirlo o cerrarlo.
es.bodyTutorial_level11: El vapor es agua en estado gaseoso. Cuando la materia está en estado gaseoso, sus partículas se mueven unas con respecto a otras y no permanecen en contacto. Están muy espaciados excepto cuando chocan, como puedes ver a continuación.
es.bodyTutorial_level2: ¡La forma del líquido ahora es diferente! El volumen es el mismo. Un líquido fluye para tomar la forma de su recipiente.
es.bodyTutorial_level3: ¿Has notado que las partículas de agua se mueven juntas hasta el final? En un líquido, las partículas de materia se mueven unas con respecto a otras, pero permanecen en contacto. Vea cómo interactúan a continuación.
es.bodyTutorial_level5: Es un emisor de frío. Afectará el estado de cualquier materia y la hará MUY FRÍA. La materia puede cambiar su estado en función de condiciones como la temperatura y la presión.
es.bodyTutorial_level6:
A diferencia de un líquido, un sólido mantiene su forma y volumen. Por eso los peces pueden ir por encima. Por lo general, es más denso que un líquido y sus partículas están muy cerca, ¡pero aún así vibran en su lugar! 

El hielo es menos denso que el agua, ¡por eso los cubos de hielo flotan! ¡Es una excepción genial!
es.bodyTutorial_level7: Cuando se expone al calor, el agua líquida se convierte en vapor (gas) y se expande para tomar el volumen del recipiente. Cuando el vapor se expone a temperaturas frías, volverá a ser líquido y caerá, ya que un líquido es generalmente más denso que un gas.
es.bodyTutorial_level7_tip1: Calor = El líquido se vuelve vapor
es.bodyTutorial_level7_tip2: Frío = El vapor se vuelve líquido
es.bodyTutorial_level9: Hay cuatro estados de la materia. ¡Aquí puedes verlos todos al mismo tiempo!
es.bodyTutorial_waterShape_FailState1: ¡Recuerda! Los líquidos fluirán para adoptar la forma de su recipiente. ¡Abre el camino!
es.bodyTutorial_waterShape_FailState2: ¡Recuerda! Los líquidos fluirán para adoptar la forma de su recipiente. ¡Cierra el camino!
es.titleCutscene_Fish_1: Estoy harto de vivir dentro de este acuario... ¡Quiero ver el OCÉANO!
es.titleCutscene_Fish_2: Si tan solo entendiera más sobre cómo funcionan los estados de la materia, sería capaz de encontrar una forma de salir de aquí...
es.titleCutscene_Fish_3: Quizá pueda meterme dentro del agua líquida de la bañera. ¡Seguro que es una buena idea!
es.titleCutsceneFinal_Fish_1: ¡Ahora sé mucho sobre los estados de la materia!
es.titleCutsceneFinal_Fish_2: Por fin puedo disfrutar nadando en esta agua líquida del océano...
es.titleCutsceneFinal_Fish_3: Creo que voy a ir al Polo Norte... ¡seguro que es una buena idea!
es.titleEnableAutomaticTTS: ¿Desea activar la conversión automática de texto a voz?
es.titleLastLevelDialogue_Fish_1: ¡Es hora de demostrar que he aprendido todo sobre los estados de la materia!
es.titleLastLevelDialogue_Fish_2: Y que no me gusta el fuego.
es.titleTapAnywhere: Mantén presionada cualquier tecla para continuar...
